channel.title: OT/ICS Threat Intel - Food & Agriculture
channel.description: Curated OT/ICS advisories, incidents and threat intelligence. Editorial by Mike Holcomb in partnership with UtilSec.

item.title: [Critical] “The Gentlemen” ransomware halts food producers — Mackay Sugar mills down for a week as Royal Foods confirms an incident
item.description: A fast-scaling ransomware operation known as The Gentlemen is hitting food and agriculture directly. In June it claimed Mackay Sugar, Australia’s second-largest raw sugar producer, shutting the Farleigh and Racecourse mills for over a week and disrupting cane haulage for more than 1,300 grower families. On 7 July the group listed Queensland food supplier Royal Foods, which has confirmed it is responding to a cyber incident. The gang ships custom tooling built to disable endpoint protection before encrypting, and it recently overtook Qilin as the month’s most prolific strain. For processors the lesson is familiar but urgent: perishable and just-in-time production means downtime spoils product and cascades through supply contracts fast, and IT-side ransomware reaches the plant floor through shared Active Directory, virtualization and ERP/MES rather than any ICS-specific exploit. - Cyber Daily / Unit 42 / ransomware trackers - Mackay Sugar Jun 2026 — Royal Foods listed 7 Jul, incident confirmed; ongoing

item.title: [Critical] “CitrixBleed” returns: NetScaler flaw exploited within 24 hours, leaking secrets from gateways that front plant and distribution remote access
item.description: Citrix patched CVE-2026-8451 on 30 June — an unauthenticated memory-overread in NetScaler ADC and Gateway appliances configured as a SAML identity provider — and researchers saw in-the-wild exploitation within 24 hours, aided by a same-day public proof-of-concept. Like the original CitrixBleed, it lets a remote attacker read appliance memory and harvest live session tokens, credentials and TLS keys. Food manufacturers and distributors often use NetScaler to publish remote access to MES, ERP, ordering and warehouse systems, so leaked secrets can hand an attacker authenticated entry toward production and logistics networks. Assume any internet-facing, unpatched NetScaler had its memory read after 1 July. - CVE-2026-8451 - Patched 30 Jun 2026 — exploited within 24h; ongoing

item.title: [High] Ransomware at Dairy Farmers of America processing plants leaks personal data
item.description: Dairy Farmers of America (DFA), one of the largest US dairy cooperatives, confirmed a ransomware attack that affected multiple manufacturing plants within its network, with a ransomware group taking credit. DFA has begun filing breach notifications — disclosing to Maine regulators that the personal information of several thousand people was exposed — and worked to restore affected operations. The case is a reminder that dairy and perishable processing are squarely in the ransomware crosshairs, where downtime threatens both food safety and highly time-sensitive product. Processors should verify IT/OT segmentation, confirm offline backups for plant systems, and prepare breach-notification and continuity plans before an incident, not during one. - The Record / Recorded Future News - Confirmed Jun 2026 — breach notification early Jul; ongoing

item.title: [Info] Food and Ag-ISAC: ransomware against the sector surges as Qilin, Akira and CL0P lead campaigns
item.description: The Food and Ag-ISAC’s ransomware analysis documents a sharp rise in attacks on food and agriculture, with a handful of groups — Qilin, Akira, CL0P, Play and Lynx — driving nearly half of recorded incidents against the sector, and separate ISAC work counting dozens of active threat actors targeting food supply chains. The through-line is that food and ag are no longer overlooked: converged IT/OT, thin margins and just-in-time supply chains make downtime expensive and pressure victims to pay. Processors should map their most disruption-sensitive lines, segment and back them up, and use ISAC intelligence to prioritize the groups and techniques actually hitting the sector. - Food and Ag-ISAC / Industrial Cyber - Report published 2026 (2025 data)

item.title: [Critical] Actively-exploited Lantronix EDS5000 flaw hands attackers root on serial-to-IP bridges used in processing and cold-chain control
item.description: CISA says CVE-2025-67038, a command-injection flaw in Lantronix EDS5000 serial-to-IP device servers, is being exploited in the wild and added it to the KEV catalog on 23 June (advisory ICSA-26-069-02). An unauthenticated attacker can inject OS commands via the username parameter and run them as root, fully controlling a device whose job is bridging legacy serial gear — PLCs, meters, refrigeration and monitoring sensors — onto IP networks. Forescout showed such bugs can even manipulate sensor readings to hide dangerous conditions — a real concern where those readings govern cold-chain temperature or process safety in food production. - CVE-2025-67038 - KEV-added 23 Jun 2026 — exploitation since 5 Apr

item.title: [Critical] Siemens urges immediate patching of RUGGEDCOM switches as a critical SINEC OS flaw (CVSS 9.8) draws a public exploit
item.description: CISA advisory ICSA-26-188-05 (published 7 July) flags a critical CVSS 9.8 vulnerability in Siemens SINEC OS before V4.0, the operating system on RUGGEDCOM industrial networking gear. A memory-corruption bug in a bundled third-party component lets a remote attacker compromise unpatched devices, and a public exploit has already been disclosed. Alongside Siemens SIMATIC controllers, RUGGEDCOM networking appears in larger food and beverage plants, sitting in the path that segments and moves control-system traffic. No confirmed in-the-wild ICS attacks are reported yet, but a public exploit plus a 9.8 score on this backbone gear warrants emergency patch prioritization. - SINEC OS < V4.0 - Published 7 Jul 2026

item.title: [High] Rockwell Automation patches flaws across Logix controllers, RSLinx and FactoryTalk used on food processing lines
item.description: Rockwell Automation released fixes for multiple vulnerabilities spanning its Logix controller family, RSLinx communications software and FactoryTalk suite — including a stack-based buffer overflow in RSLinx that could allow remote code execution. Rockwell/Allen-Bradley control gear is pervasive on food and beverage processing and packaging lines, from ControlLogix and CompactLogix processors to FactoryTalk HMIs. None of the issues are reported exploited in the wild, but the affected components sit deep in plant control and engineering workflows, so patching should be scheduled into the next sanitation or maintenance window with management-network isolation in the interim. - Rockwell / SecurityWeek - Reported Jun 2026

item.title: [Info] CISA now targets September 2026 for the final CIRCIA rule — 72-hour incident and 24-hour ransom-payment reporting ahead
item.description: CISA is expected to finalize the CIRCIA rule this fall, with reporting now targeted for around September 2026. Once effective, covered critical-infrastructure entities — food and agriculture among the 16 sectors — would have to report substantial cyber incidents within 72 hours and ransom payments within 24 hours to CISA. Given how often food and beverage firms face ransomware (see Threat Intelligence), the practical work is building an incident-classification and reporting playbook now, so a real event does not become a missed federal deadline. Firms should track the final rule text for exact covered-entity thresholds, and align it with Food and Ag-ISAC reporting practices. - Federal News Network - Final rule targeted ~Sep 2026
